summary: Italian film director (1912–2007) known for the 'Trilogy of Alienation' (L'Avventura, La Notte, L'Eclisse), all starring Monica Vitti, and for the English-language Blow-Up (1966). His films are characterized by themes of alienation, unresolved mysteries, and modernist landscapes. Blow-Up, based on a Julio Cortazar story, follows a London photographer who realizes he has photographed a murder. Died on the same day as Ingmar Bergman.

works.name: Blow-Up (1966)
works.description: Antonioni's most frequently clued film; his English-language debut. A London fashion photographer (David Hemmings) inadvertently photographs a murder in a park and becomes obsessed with proving it. Based on Julio Cortazar's short story 'Blow-Up' ('Las babas del diablo'). Scored by Herbie Hancock. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Ends with mimes pretending to play tennis in a park where the protagonist earlier caught evidence of the murder; the protagonist finally joins in with the imaginary game
examples: A mimed tennis match ends another of this director's films shortly after a character watches Jeff Beck smash a guitar.
At the end of this film, the protagonist watches two mimes act out a game of tennis in the park where he met a femme fatale played by Vanessa Redgrave.

clues.clue: A London fashion photographer (played by David Hemmings) realizes he has accidentally photographed a murder by enlarging park photographs
examples: In that film by this director, a London fashion photographer discovers that he has inadvertently photographed a murder.

The protagonist of this film, played by David Hemmings, attempts to expose a murder plot through his photographs of a couple, but he is left with only an unintelligible, magnified picture of a corpse in a park.

clues.clue: Jeff Beck of The Yardbirds smashes his guitar at a concert; the protagonist grabs the guitar neck from the mob, then discards it outside
examples: A film by this director ends with a group of mimes pretending to play tennis the morning after the protagonist catches a piece of a guitar smashed by Jeff Beck.
Herbie Hancock included a cover of Tiny Bradshaw's 'Train Kept-a-Rollin,' featuring guitarists Jeff Beck and Jimmy Page of the Yardbirds, on a soundtrack for an Italian director.

clues.clue: Two nude women (including 19-year-old Jane Birkin and Gillian Hills) wrestle on an overturned clothes rack, then on a large sheet of purple paper
examples: This director infamously filmed two nude women struggling first on an overturned clothes rack, then on a giant sheet of paper, in a fight featuring a nineteen-year-old Jane Birkin.
The protagonist of this film romps around on a purple strip of paper with two nude women played by Gillian Hills and Jane Birkin.

clues.clue: The protagonist buys a large propeller for eight pounds from an antique shop with many statues; later leaves it behind
examples: In another film by this director, the protagonist enters an art shop with many statues, and pays eight pounds for a large propeller.
One character buys a propeller for eight pounds, but he ends up leaving it behind.

clues.clue: Based on Julio Cortazar's short story 'Las babas del diablo' / 'Blow-Up'; adapted by Antonioni
examples: For 10 points, name this Italian director who adapted a Julio Cortazar story into his film Blow-Up.
That story, 'Las Babas del Diablo', was adapted into Michelangelo Antonioni's film Blow-Up.

clues.clue: Scored by Herbie Hancock — his first English-language film score
examples: Herbie Hancock scored his first English-language film, Blow-Up, which was based on a short story by Julio Cortazar.

clues.clue: The Cortazar original follows a French-Chilean translator-photographer named Roberto Michel who photographs a woman seducing a teenage boy on behalf of a man in a car
examples: The narrator of another of his stories compares the movement of clouds to a 'spell of weeping reversed'... The narrator realizes that the blonde he saw in the park was actually luring a boy into the car of the man in the grey hat.

clues.clue: Francis Ford Coppola dismissed Watergate's influence on The Conversation and instead credited Blow-Up as his inspiration; both films involve surveillance and voyeurism
examples: Though it won at Cannes, this film was nominated but failed to win Best Picture to a sequel film made by the same director, who dismissed the influence of Watergate and instead credited Antonioni's Blow-Up.

works.name: L'Avventura (1960)
works.description: The first film of Antonioni's 'Trilogy of Alienation.' Anna vanishes during a yacht trip to the Aeolian Islands; the mystery is never resolved as her boyfriend Sandro and best friend Claudia (Monica Vitti) fall in love while searching for her. Received a Special Jury Prize at Cannes 1960 despite being booed. Placed 2nd in the 1962 Sight & Sound poll. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Anna disappears during a yachting/boating trip to the Aeolian Islands; her disappearance is never solved; Claudia and Sandro fall in love while searching for her
examples: Claudia and Sandro embrace in that film by this director after failing to solve Anna's disappearance during a boating trip.
This Antonioni film, to which La Notte is the spiritual successor, opens when Anna, Claudia, and Sandro go on a yachting trip, only for Anna to disappear.

clues.clue: Ends with Claudia stroking Sandro's weeping head on a bench; Mount Etna looms in the background
examples: This film ends with that woman stroking a man's hair as he weeps on a bench, with Mount Etna looming in the distance.
ends with a split shot between a building and a distant view of Mount Etna as a woman consoles her crying lover

clues.clue: Won the Special Jury Prize at Cannes 1960 for 'seeking to create a new film language' despite being booed at its premiere; placed 2nd in the 1962 Sight & Sound poll behind Citizen Kane
examples: A film by this director placed second behind Citizen Kane in the 1962 Sight & Sound poll, and received a Special Jury Prize 'for seeking to create a new film language' at the 1960 Cannes festival, despite being booed at its premiere there.

clues.clue: At the press conference following the premiere, Antonioni declared himself not a moralist and said the tragedy 'stems directly from a miserable erotic impulse'
examples: At a press conference following this film's premiere, its director declared himself not to be a moralist and uninterested in resolving the 'split between moral man and scientific man.'

clues.clue: Stars Monica Vitti as Claudia; Sandro is played by Gabriele Ferzetti; set partly on the volcanic Aeolian Islands

examples: Pauline Kael criticizes this film's spiritual sequel and reflects on her year trying to 'persuade, goad, and even shame people into seeing' this film in her essay 'The Come-Dressed-As-the-Sick-Soul-of-Europe Parties.'

clues.clue: Sofia Coppola used rain masking dialogue in Lost in Translation in a style inspired by Antonioni's trilogy of alienation
examples: The two protagonists of Lost in Translation whisper a conversation unheard by the viewer in a style inspired by the use of rain to mask dialogue in this director's 'trilogy of alienation.'

works.name: La Notte (1961)
works.description: The second film in the Trilogy of Alienation. Follows the dissolving marriage of Giovanni and Lidia over the course of one day. Stars Marcello Mastroianni and Jeanne Moreau. Criticized by Manny Farber in 'White Elephant Art vs. Termite Art.' Wikipedia

examples: Farber criticizes this Italian director's film La Notte as 'an example of the evils of continuity.'

works.name: L'Eclisse (1962)
works.description: The third film in the Trilogy of Alienation. Follows Vittoria, a translator, who begins an affair with Piero after meeting him at a stock exchange. Famous for its enigmatic open ending — just shots of a streetlight where the couple agreed to meet. Stars Monica Vitti. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Follows Vittoria, a translator, who begins an affair with Piero after meeting him at the stock exchange
examples: A film by this director follows Vittoria, a translator who begins an affair with Piero after meeting him at a stock exchange.

clues.clue: Ends with shots of a streetlight where a man and woman had agreed to meet; Terence McNally called it 'the empty requiem for two dead souls'
examples: Terence McNally once described the ending to one of this director's films as 'the empty requiem for two dead souls,' since it largely consists of shots of a streetlight, where a man and a woman had agreed to meet to continue their affair.

clues.clue: Sandro spills an inkwell over a student's architectural sketch in a scene

works.name: The Passenger (1975)
works.description: Antonioni's late-career masterwork starring Jack Nicholson as reporter David Locke, who assumes a dead man's identity. Famous for its seven-minute tracking shot that moves through a Spanish hotel window, circles the plaza, and returns to reveal the protagonist's death. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Penultimate shot: a seven-minute-long tracking shot starting in a hotel room, moving out the window and circling back to reveal hitmen killing reporter David Locke
examples: The penultimate shot of one of this director's films is a seven-minute-long tracking shot starting in a hotel room, going out the window, and turning around and going back into the hotel room.
Near the end of a film by this director, the camera appears to move between the bars of a Spanish hotel's window and circle back as hitmen kill reporter David Locke.

works.name: Zabriskie Point (1970)
works.description: Antonioni's only American film. Critically derided — included in The Fifty Worst Films of All Time. Ends with protagonist Daria imagining a modernist house in the desert exploding in slow motion. Pink Floyd contributed to the score. Wikipedia

The book The Fifty Worst Films of All Time includes this director's only American film, which ends with household items flying through the air as a modernist house blows up.

comprehensive_summary:
Michelangelo Antonioni (1912–2007) was an Italian filmmaker celebrated for his exploration of alienation, modern ennui, and unresolved ambiguity. He is most closely associated with his 'Trilogy of Alienation' — L'Avventura (1960), La Notte (1961), and L'Eclisse (1962) — all starring Monica Vitti. Seymour Chatman extended this to a tetralogy including Red Desert (1964). Antonioni died on the same day as Ingmar Bergman in 2007, sparking critical debate about their respective styles.

L'Avventura (1960) is Antonioni's most critically praised work. Anna, Claudia (Monica Vitti), and Sandro (Gabriele Ferzetti) go on a yacht trip to the volcanic Aeolian Islands; Anna disappears and is never found. The rest of the film follows Claudia and Sandro as they fall into an affair while ostensibly searching for Anna. The film ends with Claudia stroking Sandro's weeping head on a bench, with Mount Etna looming in the background. It was booed at its Cannes premiere but won the Special Jury Prize 'for seeking to create a new film language.' It placed second behind Citizen Kane in the 1962 Sight & Sound poll. Pauline Kael wrote about her struggle to get people to see it in her essay 'The Come-Dressed-As-the-Sick-Soul-of-Europe Parties.' Sofia Coppola used the technique of rain masking dialogue in Lost in Translation as a homage to the trilogy's style.

La Notte (1961), the middle part of the trilogy, follows the dissolving marriage of Giovanni and Lidia over one day. It stars Marcello Mastroianni and Jeanne Moreau, and ends with Mastroianni trying to make love to Moreau in a golf course bunker. Manny Farber attacked it in his essay 'White Elephant Art vs. Termite Art' as 'an example of the evils of continuity.'

L'Eclisse (1962), the third part, follows Vittoria (Monica Vitti), a translator, who begins an affair with Piero (Alain Delon) after meeting him at the stock exchange. The film's famous ending consists solely of shots of a streetlight where the couple agreed to meet — Terence McNally called it 'the empty requiem for two dead souls.'

Blow-Up (1966) is Antonioni's most frequently clued film and his most widely seen work. A London fashion photographer (David Hemmings) inadvertently photographs a murder in a park when he captures a couple embracing. He discovers the murder only after enlarging his photos. After a Yardbirds concert (where Jeff Beck smashes his guitar and the protagonist grabs the guitar neck before discarding it), the film ends with the photographer watching mimes play an imaginary tennis match in the park and eventually joining in — suggesting the reality of the murder has dissolved. Along the way, two nude women (including 19-year-old Jane Birkin and Gillian Hills) wrestle on an overturned clothes rack and then on a large purple sheet of paper. The protagonist also buys a large propeller for eight pounds from an antique shop. Scored by Herbie Hancock (his first English-language score), the film was based on Julio Cortazar's short story 'Las babas del diablo.' The femme fatale was played by Vanessa Redgrave. Francis Ford Coppola cited Blow-Up rather than Watergate as the inspiration for The Conversation.

Zabriskie Point (1970), Antonioni's only American film, is critically derided — included in The Fifty Worst Films of All Time. It ends with protagonist Daria imagining a modernist house in the desert exploding, with household items flying through the air in slow motion. Pink Floyd's 'Us and Them' was based on their work for this film's score. The Passenger (1975) stars Jack Nicholson as reporter David Locke. It is famous for its seven-minute tracking shot that begins in a Spanish hotel room, moves through the window bars into the courtyard, circles the plaza, and returns to reveal hitmen killing Locke — all in one uncut movement. Nicholson's character also picks up a young architecture student first shown reading in a Gaudí building.

recursive_suggestions: Blow-Up (1966) — the most frequently clued Antonioni film; Cortazar's story 'Las babas del diablo' also clued separately. Search: 'Blow-Up', category: Fine Arts
L'Avventura — second-most clued; often appears as a bonus answer. Search: 'L'Avventura', category: Fine Arts
Monica Vitti — starred in all of the Trilogy of Alienation films; likely has her own clue set. Search: 'Monica Vitti', category: Fine Arts
